wow! blondie finally in malaysia! so exciting!! welcome!! 欢迎 😃😃👏👏actually, there is no such thing as pure __ (whatever race) food here. you would find a mixture of hybrid fusion of different influences in all these food. you have malay and india influences in the local chinese food; chinese influences in both malay or indian food etc.. it's just one huge melting pot of food cultures heaven here. btw, i think the roti tsunami is a bit of an overkill. i personally prefer the traditional, conventional way - just the bread with the sauce.. you would find that malaysians like this kind of crazy stuff- to "flood" their food with different sauces, and to overflow their plate with all sort of combo.. it's kind of messy and not necessarily the best way to enjoying the food. but then again, people are free to eat in whatever way they enjoy the best. and you must have those huge blood clams (si ham) in the char kuey teow!! anyway, have a great trip and hope you enjoy the food here (and come again!) 😉🙂🙂

as a penang chinese guy, three dishes that are on top of everything, they are keow teow teng (pork soup keow teow), wantan mee (pork mince wantan with egg noodle), and the last one is char keow teow.they are the three kings.then later there are hokkien mee (prawn soup noodle), asam laksa and curry noodle, they are the three dukes.the the less famous but also delicious such as keow chap (pork, duck, chicken based soup noodles), jawa mee (tomato soup noodle), char tua pan (wok fried big sizes noodle served with gravy soup, pork and prawn based food). these are the three counts. the remaining rest as above than average food from chinese.as for malays and indians food, they are colourful and coconut milk based, and very tasty.so, after all delicious penang food, maybe your body become so hot, you can drink green coconut water too. ahha

you’ve got the origins of chicken rice a bit mixed up. it was the hainanese migrants who brought the dish from hainan to singapore, malaysia, and other places. they arrived later than other chinese groups, such as those from quanzhou, chaoshan, and other coastal areas. by the time the hainanese came, the coolie trade was already saturated, and opportunities in commerce—like the tea and chinese goods trade—were limited. as a result, many of them found work as cooks for the british colonial households. this eventually led them to venture into the food and beverage industry.their entry into this space gave rise to several uniquely hainanese culinary creations that are distinct to singapore and malaysia. among them was their localized version of wenchang chicken, adapted using local poultry and ingredients. naturally, the dish evolved differently in various regions due to the availability of ingredients and local preferences.
